Graduation Project · Hacettepe University

Optimization of Neural Networks for
Satellite Imagery-Based Disaster Risk Monitoring

SatRisk-Net near real-time wildfire segmentation, on the edge.
Supervisor: Asst. Prof. Dr. Selma Dilek
Emre Erdoğan·Emre Yeğin·Özgün Serergün Koca·Mustafa Ege·Kağan Canerik
Hacettepe
University
Demo Video
01

Introduction · The Crisis

why this matters

Wildfires can spread quickly and damage large areas, so affected regions must be identified as early as possible. Satellite imagery provides a powerful way to observe disasters from above, but large satellite images are difficult to process quickly with traditional cloud-based workflows. In this project, burned areas are segmented from satellite images using deep learning models. The trained models are then optimized for faster and lighter inference on edge devices such as NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano.

Datasets · open, multi-region, human-verified

HLS Burn Scars
NASA · HLS · 6 bands · 512² tiles
Harmonized Landsat-Sentinel imagery with human-verified burn-scar masks. Our primary benchmark.
CEMS
Copernicus EMS · Sentinel-2
Real European emergency-mapping events. Tests cross-region generalization on operational data.
Burned Area
Public · Sentinel-2 · multispectral
Used for hyperparameter sensitivity analysis — small set, varied terrain, fast iteration.
3 × 3
Three segmentation networks — U-Net, DeepLabV3+, SegFormer — evaluated on three independent wildfire datasets (HLS Burn Scars, CEMS, Burned Area), then compressed to run live on an NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano with TensorRT.
Why we moved inference to the edge
A traditional ground pipeline can take hours from satellite pass to actionable map. Running models on edge devices near the data source can reduce latency and bandwidth usage.
02

Model Training

three architectures · one pipeline

We benchmark U-Net (ResNet-18), DeepLabV3+ (ResNet-50), and SegFormer-B0 under one shared pipeline so the architectures — not the plumbing — decide the winner. All three are adapted from RGB to 6-band HLS imagery (B, G, R, NIR, SWIR-1, SWIR-2), trained with masked BCE / Dice / Focal losses, AdamW + AMP, and identical split protocols across every dataset.

Step 1
Dataset
Sentinel-2 / HLS GeoTIFF · load · cloud-mask · locked test split
Step 2
Preprocessing
Tile 512² · per-band 2–98% normalize · hflip + vflip + rot90 augment
Step 3
Training
U-Net · DeepLabV3+ · SegFormer · AdamW + AMP · BCE + Dice masked · warmup + cosine · grad clip 1.0
Step 4
Hyperparameter Search
LR · loss · grad-clip · augmentation · scheduler ablations
Step 5
Validation & IoU Evaluation
SWA + TTA + Ensemble · val-tuned threshold · held-out test + TP/FP/FN overlays

Training strategy

  • One pipeline, fair comparison. Same splits, same preprocessing, same metric — only the architecture changes.
  • Class imbalance is the hard part. Disaster pixels are rare; we use Dice / Focal alongside BCE to keep the gradient honest.
  • Controlled ablations. LR ∈ {5e-5, 1e-4, 2e-4}, gradient clipping, encoder freezing, AMP, scheduler choice — each varied independently.
  • Reproducibility first. Locked seeds, locked HF test split, W&B-tracked runs, deterministic preprocessing.
  • 30 epochs, best-val checkpoint. Selected purely on validation IoU before touching the test set.
Validation IoU — HLS Burn Scars · 30 epochsU-NetDeepLabV3+SegFormer-B0
All three architectures converge into the 0.80 – 0.83 band on HLS. Transformer (SegFormer) leads on validation; CNNs catch up on test (see §04).
03

Results

three datasets · test set iou

Three segmentation models were evaluated on three wildfire datasets. Best architecture is dataset-dependent: U-Net leads on the smaller CEMS, DeepLabV3+ on the larger HLS. SegFormer, which collapsed to 0.11 IoU under a naive from-scratch baseline on CEMS, was recovered to ≥ 0.80 across both datasets through patch-embed surgery (3→12 ch) and transformer-appropriate fine-tuning.

Held-out scenes, side-by-side. All models recover the dominant burn scar; differences emerge at edges and small detached patches.
CEMS · U-Net test predictions
Sentinel-2 RGB · ground truth · U-Net prediction · overlay (TP green, FP red, FN blue). Reaches 0.8280 IoU on the operational CEMS split.

04

Edge Optimization

jetson orin nano · tensorrt

Pruning was applied to make the models lighter and faster on Jetson. Structured pruning removes channel-level parts of the network and gave the best accuracy–speed trade-off, especially for U-Net and DeepLabV3+.

How we measured both passes
  1. Host GPU. Pruning + reduced precision compared against the unpruned PyTorch baseline.
  2. On-device. Best variants exported via ONNX, compiled into a TensorRT engine, re-benchmarked on the Orin Nano at 7 W – MAXN.
Key Finding Structured pruning gave the best Jetson accuracy–speed trade-off.
NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano
A compact edge-AI device for running neural networks locally. Reduces cloud dependency and supports faster inference near the data source.
PyTorch · pruning & precision sweep (host GPU)
 Baseline Unstructured 20% Structured 10% Structured 25% (aggr fp32) fp16
Structured pruning (25%, aggressive fp32) is the consistent winner across U-Net and DeepLabV3+. SegFormer is largely insensitive — pruning gives no speed-up but no accuracy loss either.
On-device · Python baseline vs TensorRT engine (Jetson Orin Nano)
 Python Baseline TensorRT Engine
Converting to TensorRT brings ~3× lower latency across all architectures with no accuracy loss. DeepLabV3+ benefits most in absolute terms (180 → 60 ms / tile), making it newly viable for near real-time use on the Orin Nano.
05

Interface & Future Vision

website · what's next

The trained models are wrapped in a Leaflet + React web interface so anyone can browse predictions on a world map, compare architectures side-by-side, toggle the ground truth overlay, and inspect the per-region IoU. The backend serves cached predictions for every Copernicus EMS event we evaluated.

DeepLabV3+ · Pedrógão Grande, Portugal IoU 0.9258
DeepLabV3+ · Bastia, Corsica IoU 0.8693

What a user can do

  • Pick a region from any indexed wildfire event.
  • Switch the model — U-Net / DeepLabV3+ / SegFormer — and watch the prediction update.
  • Toggle ground-truth overlay and read the per-region IoU live.
  • Compare segmentation outputs across different architectures .
A
Sensor. Sentinel-2 / Landsat captures multispectral imagery on every orbital pass.
B
Edge inference. Running models on edge devices near the data source can reduce latency and bandwidth usage.
C
Near real-time alert. Only compact georeferenced masks and alert payloads are transmitted, significantly reducing bandwidth compared to raw satellite imagery.
Toward a scalable disaster monitoring platform

Multi-hazard. The pipeline is designed to be adaptable to multiple hazard types, including floods, landslides, and post-earthquake damage assessment, through retraining on domain-specific datasets.

Live data. Near real-time Sentinel-2 and Sentinel-1 data integration enables faster processing from acquisition to alert generation, even under intermittent connectivity.

Stack PyTorch TensorRT ONNX Jetson Orin Nano Rasterio FastAPI React Leaflet
